q: Walk me through the full `UIViewController` lifecycle and explain what work belongs in each phase.
a: The controller starts with `loadView`, which creates the root view, followed by `viewDidLoad`, which runs exactly once after the view hierarchy is in memory, so that's where I do one-time setup like wiring outlets, registering cells, and creating subviews. Then `viewWillAppear` runs every time the view is about to become visible, which is good for refreshing data or starting observers, and `viewDidAppear` is where I start animations or analytics that need the view actually on screen. On the way out you get `viewWillDisappear` and `viewDidDisappear` for pausing work and tearing down. Layout callbacks like `viewWillLayoutSubviews` and `viewDidLayoutSubviews` are interleaved and can fire many times, so I never put one-time setup there.
d: The key misconception is treating `viewDidLoad` as 'once per appearance' — it runs once per view load, but if the view is purged under memory pressure (pre-iOS 6 behavior, now rare) it can run again. `viewWillAppear`/`viewDidAppear` are paired with `viewWillDisappear`/`viewDidDisappear` and fire on every transition, including when returning from a pushed controller, so anything started in `willAppear` must be balanced in `willDisappear` to avoid leaks. Geometry is NOT reliable in `viewDidLoad` because Auto Layout hasn't resolved frames yet; final frames are only trustworthy in `viewDidLayoutSubviews`, which itself can be called multiple times per appearance (rotation, keyboard, safe area changes). Appearance callbacks are driven by the containing controller (e.g. `UINavigationController`) and respect `beginAppearanceTransition`. During an interactive pop that is cancelled, you can get `viewWillDisappear` followed by `viewWillAppear` without an actual disappearance, so state machines built naively on these callbacks can desync. Setting `definesPresentationContext` or using `UIModalPresentationStyle` like `.overCurrentContext` changes whether the presenter receives disappear callbacks at all.
f: Trap: A candidate says 'I'll read `view.frame` in `viewDidLoad` to size a subview.' Why is that wrong? Because frames aren't laid out yet in `viewDidLoad`; the view typically has its nib/storyboard size or zero. Use Auto Layout constraints, or read frames in `viewDidLayoutSubviews`.

q: What is the difference between `loadView` and `viewDidLoad`, and when would you override `loadView`?
a: `loadView` is the method whose job is to actually create the root view and assign it to `self.view` — if you don't override it, the default implementation loads from a nib/storyboard or creates a plain `UIView`. `viewDidLoad` runs after `loadView` has finished and the view exists, so it's for configuring that view, not creating it. I override `loadView` when I'm building a controller fully in code and want a custom root view class, because doing it there avoids ever instantiating the default view. The cardinal rule is never call `super.loadView()` if you're providing your own view, and never touch `self.view` inside `loadView` before assigning it, because accessing `self.view` triggers `loadView` recursively.
d: Accessing the `view` property is what lazily triggers the load cycle: the getter checks if `view` is nil and, if so, calls `loadView` then `viewDidLoad`. This is why referencing `self.view` from an `init` or from inside `loadView` itself causes premature or recursive loading and subtle bugs. Overriding `loadView` to build a custom root view is the cleanest pattern for code-only controllers because it avoids the wasted allocation of the default `UIView` and gives you a typed root view (a common pattern is a separate `MyView` class exposed via a typed `view` computed property). If you load from a nib, you should NOT override `loadView` — the nib loading machinery does it for you and sets up outlets. A frequent senior mistake is mixing both: overriding `loadView` while also having a storyboard scene, which leads to the storyboard view being discarded. Note `loadViewIfNeeded()` (iOS 9+) lets you force the load without making the view appear, useful for pre-warming.
f: Trap: 'I'll set up my custom root view by adding subviews in `loadView` to `self.view`.' What's the danger? Reading `self.view` inside `loadView` before you've assigned it re-enters `loadView`. Create a local view, configure it, then assign `self.view = customView` last (or do subview setup in `viewDidLoad`).

q: Explain the responder chain and how a touch or action message travels through it.
a: The responder chain is a linked list of `UIResponder` objects — views, view controllers, the window, and the application — connected by the `next` property, and UIKit walks it to find an object that can handle an event or action. For touches, the chain starts at the hit-tested view and bubbles up through superviews, then the view's controller, the window, and finally the app delegate if nothing handles it. For control actions sent with a `nil` target, UIKit also walks this chain looking for the first responder that implements the selector, which is how `UIMenuController` and `sendAction(_:to:nil:...)` work. Understanding `next` is key: a view's `next` is usually its superview, but if the view is a controller's root view, its `next` is the view controller.
d: `next` is computed, not stored: `UIView.next` returns its managing `UIViewController` if it has one, otherwise its superview; a controller's `next` is its view's superview (or the next controller in a containment chain); the window's `next` is the application; the app's `next` is the app delegate (and on iOS 13+ the scene/responder hierarchy participates). The 'first responder' is the responder that currently has focus for keyboard/text input — `becomeFirstResponder`/`resignFirstResponder` move it — and `nil`-targeted actions begin their search there, not at the hit-tested view. A subtle point: a responder can decline an event by NOT overriding the relevant method, in which case UIKit's default `UIResponder` implementation forwards to `next` automatically; if you override `touchesBegan` and forget to call `super`, you silently break the chain. Editing menu commands, undo manager lookup, and keyboard shortcut (`UIKeyCommand`) resolution all traverse this same chain. The chain only includes objects actually attached to the active window hierarchy — a view not in a window has a truncated chain.
f: Trap: 'Overriding `touchesBegan` in a view always consumes the touch.' True? No. If you don't forward to `super`, you may break the default forwarding to `next`. To pass touches along you must call `super.touchesBegan` (or not override at all); consuming vs forwarding is about whether you call super.

q: How does hit testing work? Describe `hitTest(_:with:)` and `point(inside:with:)`.
a: Hit testing is how UIKit decides which view receives a touch. When a touch lands, UIKit calls `hitTest(_:with:)` on the window, which recurses down the view tree: for each view it calls `point(inside:with:)` to check whether the point is inside that view, and if so it asks the subviews — in reverse order, so topmost first — and returns the deepest descendant that contains the point. The view returned becomes the hit-tested view and the start of the responder chain. I override `hitTest` or `point(inside:)` when I need to expand a tappable area beyond bounds, or to make a view transparent to touches while still drawing.
d: `hitTest` returns nil — pruning that subtree — if the view is hidden, has `isUserInteractionEnabled == false`, or `alpha` less than ~0.01, or if `point(inside:)` returns false. Crucially, `clipsToBounds` does NOT affect hit testing geometry, but `point(inside:)` uses `bounds`, so a subview drawn outside its parent's bounds won't receive touches even if visible — a classic bug fixed by overriding `point(inside:)` on the parent to enlarge the hittable region. Subviews are tested in reverse `subviews` array order because that matches z-order (last added is on top). A common pattern for a 'pass-through' overlay is to override `hitTest` to return nil (or the view behind) when the point isn't over an interactive child. Because hit testing runs once at touch-down to establish the responder, moving a view mid-gesture doesn't re-route the already-tracking touch. Performance matters: `hitTest` runs synchronously on the main thread for every touch sequence, so expensive overrides hurt scrolling responsiveness.
f: Trap: 'A button placed outside its superview's bounds still works as long as it's visible.' Right? No — even if it renders (when `clipsToBounds` is false), the parent's `point(inside:)` returns false for that region, so hit testing never reaches the button. Override the parent's `point(inside:)` or `hitTest`.

q: What is the Auto Layout engine doing under the hood? Mention the constraint solver.
a: Auto Layout is a constraint-satisfaction system built on a variant of the Cassowary linear constraint solver. You describe relationships between view geometry as linear equations and inequalities — each with a priority — and the engine solves for the view frames that satisfy all required constraints while best satisfying the optional ones. The solver runs incrementally: when you change or add a constraint it doesn't re-solve everything from scratch, it updates the affected part of the system. The output is concrete `frame` values that get applied during the layout pass.
d: Cassowary solves systems of linear constraints using an incremental simplex method, which is why it handles thousands of constraints efficiently and supports 'edit' variables for live updates like dragging. Constraints have priorities from 1 to 1000; 1000 is required and must be satisfied, while priorities below 1000 are optional and the solver minimizes the weighted error across them. The engine works in two conceptual stages tied to the run loop: `updateConstraints` (rarely overridden) lets you batch constraint changes, then the deferred layout pass computes frames via `layoutSubviews`. Conflicts arise when two required constraints can't both hold — at runtime UIKit breaks one and logs `UIViewAlertForUnsatisfiableConstraints`, choosing somewhat arbitrarily, which is why ambiguous layouts produce nondeterministic results. Internally each view participates via its `NSLayoutConstraint` objects and the engine translates `intrinsicContentSize`, content hugging, and compression resistance into additional implicit constraints. `translatesAutoresizingMaskIntoConstraints` must be false for views you constrain manually, otherwise UIKit synthesizes conflicting constraints from the autoresizing mask.
f: Trap: 'Setting two equal-priority required width constraints to different values just picks the bigger one.' Correct? No — two conflicting required (1000) constraints are unsatisfiable; UIKit breaks one nondeterministically and logs a warning. Use different priorities to express intent.

q: Explain content hugging vs compression resistance and how they relate to intrinsic content size.
a: Intrinsic content size is the natural size a view wants based on its content, like a label sizing to its text. Content hugging priority controls how much a view resists growing larger than its intrinsic size — high hugging means 'don't stretch me.' Compression resistance controls how much it resists shrinking below its intrinsic size — high resistance means 'don't clip me.' When two views compete for space in a stack or row, the one with lower hugging stretches and the one with lower compression resistance gets squeezed first. I tune these priorities to decide which label truncates and which one expands.
d: These are implemented as implicit constraints derived from `intrinsicContentSize`: hugging is an inequality `width <= intrinsicWidth` at the hugging priority, and compression resistance is `width >= intrinsicWidth` at the resistance priority. Default values are 250 for hugging and 750 for compression resistance, which is why, all else equal, views prefer to grow rather than clip. If `intrinsicContentSize` returns `UIView.noIntrinsicMetric` for an axis, no implicit constraint is generated for that axis and these priorities have no effect there. Custom views must override `intrinsicContentSize` and call `invalidateIntrinsicContentSize()` when their content changes (e.g. text set), otherwise Auto Layout uses a stale size. A classic two-label problem — one truncating, one wrapping — is solved purely by adjusting relative hugging/compression priorities, not by adding constraints. Note that multi-line labels need `preferredMaxLayoutWidth` set correctly (UIKit does this automatically when width is constrained) for intrinsic height to be computed right, which is a frequent source of wrong heights in self-sizing cells.
f: Trap: 'Raising content hugging makes a label grow to fill space.' Right? Backwards — high hugging makes it resist growing (hug its content tightly). Lower hugging on the view you want to stretch.

q: Distinguish `setNeedsLayout`, `layoutIfNeeded`, and `layoutSubviews`. When does each run?
a: `setNeedsLayout` just marks a view as needing layout and returns immediately — the actual layout happens later, coalesced, during the next layout pass in the run loop. `layoutIfNeeded` forces that pending layout to happen synchronously right now, walking up to the top dirty ancestor and laying out the subtree. `layoutSubviews` is the method UIKit calls during the layout pass where you position subviews; you never call it directly, you trigger it via `setNeedsLayout`. So the pattern is: mark dirty with `setNeedsLayout`, optionally flush with `layoutIfNeeded`, and override `layoutSubviews` to do the work.
d: The layout pass is deferred and batched: marking a view dirty sets a flag, and UIKit performs the layout pass once per run-loop iteration before drawing, so multiple `setNeedsLayout` calls collapse into a single `layoutSubviews`. Auto Layout itself drives `layoutSubviews` — the engine solves constraints and then calls `layoutSubviews` so you (or UIKit) can apply the resulting frames; if you override it with manual frame math, call `super.layoutSubviews()` first so Auto Layout-applied frames are in place. `layoutIfNeeded` is essential when animating constraint changes: you change the constant, then call `layoutIfNeeded` inside the `UIView.animate` block so the frame change is animated rather than snapping. A common bug is calling `layoutIfNeeded` on a view not yet in the window hierarchy, which can produce zero or wrong frames. `layoutSubviews` can be called surprisingly often — on bounds changes, scrolling (every frame for scroll views), rotation, and subview add/remove — so heavy work there tanks performance. The order is: `updateConstraints` (bottom-up) then the layout pass with `layoutSubviews` (top-down).
f: Trap: 'To animate a constraint change, just change the constant inside `UIView.animate`.' Enough? No — you must call `layoutIfNeeded()` inside the animation block so the layout pass runs and the frame change interpolates. Changing the constant alone animates nothing visible until the next implicit pass.

q: Describe the run loop's relationship to the layout pass, the display pass, and animations.
a: UIKit defers visual work to the end of each run-loop iteration through a CoreAnimation transaction commit. During that commit there's a layout pass, where dirty views get `layoutSubviews`, followed by a display pass, where views needing redraw get `draw(_:)` to update their layer contents, and then the assembled layer tree is handed to the render server for compositing. That's why calls like `setNeedsLayout` and `setNeedsDisplay` are cheap — they just set flags that get serviced once per cycle. Animations are also part of this: implicit animations and `UIView.animate` schedule changes that the render server interpolates on its own process.
d: Each main-thread run-loop pass ends with `CATransaction` commit driven by observers registered on the main run loop (`CFRunLoopObserver` at `kCFRunLoopBeforeWaiting`). The order within commit is: (1) layout — Auto Layout solves and `layoutSubviews` is called top-down for dirty views; (2) display — `draw(_:)` / `display` runs for views/layers flagged with `setNeedsDisplay`, producing backing-store bitmaps; (3) prepare/commit — the layer tree (including animations) is encoded and sent over IPC to the `backboardd`/render server which composites and drives the actual animation frames. Because animation runs in the render server, your app's main thread can be busy and a committed animation still progresses (though new commits stall). This separation is why `presentation layer` reflects what's on screen mid-animation while the `model layer` holds the final values. Forcing synchronous layout (`layoutIfNeeded`) or display mid-cycle is allowed but bypasses coalescing. Off-main-thread mutation breaks this because the transaction machinery and the view's nonatomic layer state assume single-threaded access.
f: Trap: 'My animation freezes if the main thread blocks for 2 seconds.' Always? Not necessarily — an already-committed Core Animation runs in the render server and keeps going; what stalls is touch handling and new commits. A blocked main thread freezes input, not an in-flight CA animation.

q: What's the difference between `setNeedsDisplay`/`draw(_:)` and the layout system?
a: `draw(_:)` is about pixels — it's where you do custom Core Graphics drawing into the view's backing store, and you trigger it by calling `setNeedsDisplay`, which marks the view for redraw in the next display pass. Layout, by contrast, is about positioning and sizing subviews via `layoutSubviews`. They're independent: changing a frame doesn't necessarily require redrawing content, and redrawing content doesn't move subviews. I only override `draw(_:)` for genuinely custom rendering, because it allocates a CPU-backed bitmap; for most cases I prefer `CALayer` properties or images, which the GPU handles more cheaply.
d: Overriding `draw(_:)` makes UIKit allocate a backing bitmap sized to bounds times `contentsScale`, and your drawing happens on the CPU into that bitmap, which is then uploaded as a texture — expensive in memory and time, especially for large or frequently invalidated views. If you don't override `draw(_:)` and the view has no custom content, UIKit can skip allocating a backing store entirely, letting the layer composite cheaply. `setNeedsDisplay` coalesces like `setNeedsLayout` — multiple calls cause one redraw per cycle — and `setNeedsDisplay(_:)` with a rect can limit the redrawn region, though many implementations ignore the rect. A misconception is that resizing a view re-runs `draw(_:)`; by default the layer just stretches its existing contents according to `contentsGravity` unless `contentMode` is `.redraw`, which forces re-display on bounds change. For animated or vector-ish content, `CAShapeLayer`, `CGGradientLayer`, or pre-rendered `UIImage`s avoid the CPU bitmap cost. `draw(_:)` runs on the main thread within the display pass, so heavy drawing janks the UI; `CALayer`'s `drawAsync`/`UIGraphicsImageRenderer` off the main thread are alternatives.
f: Trap: 'Resizing a custom-drawn view automatically calls `draw(_:)` again.' Always? No — by default the layer stretches existing contents. You must set `contentMode = .redraw` (or call `setNeedsDisplay` on bounds change) to force re-drawing on resize.

q: Explain frame vs bounds vs center, and how they interact with transforms.
a: `bounds` is the view's own coordinate system — its origin is usually zero and its size is the view's size in its own space. `frame` is the view's rectangle in its superview's coordinate system, derived from bounds, center, and transform. `center` is the position of the bounds' center point, also in the superview's coordinates. The key relationship is that frame is a computed value: it's essentially the bounds rotated/scaled by the transform and positioned by center, so when a non-identity transform is applied, `frame` becomes the bounding box of the transformed view and is no longer meaningful to set directly.
d: With the identity transform, `frame.size == bounds.size` and `frame.origin = center - bounds.size/2` (accounting for `anchorPoint`, default 0.5,0.5). When you set a transform — say a 45-degree rotation — `bounds` and `center` are unchanged but `frame` reports the axis-aligned bounding rectangle that encloses the rotated view, so reading or setting `frame` under a transform is misleading and Apple explicitly warns against it. Changing `bounds.origin` scrolls the view's content (this is exactly how `UIScrollView` works — it just shifts its bounds origin, repositioning subviews without moving itself). `anchorPoint` (a layer property) shifts the point that `center`/`position` refers to and the pivot for transforms; moving it without compensating shifts the view. Because frame is computed, animating frame vs animating bounds+center can differ subtly. Auto Layout sets frame for you, so under Auto Layout you generally read these rather than set them, and setting frame directly on an Auto-Layout-managed view gets overwritten at the next layout pass.
f: Trap: 'I'll set `view.frame` to reposition a rotated view.' Problem? Under a non-identity transform, `frame` is the bounding box, not the real geometry — setting it produces wrong results. Move it via `center` (and `bounds` for size), not `frame`.

q: What is the relationship between a `UIView` and its `CALayer`?
a: Every `UIView` is backed by a `CALayer` accessible via the `layer` property, and the view is essentially a thin responder/event-handling wrapper around that layer, which does the actual rendering and compositing. Geometric properties like frame, bounds, and transform on the view are really projecting onto the layer underneath. The big practical differences are that layers don't participate in the responder chain or Auto Layout, and layers have implicit animations by default whereas views disable them outside animation blocks. So when I want lightweight visual elements without touch handling, I use layers directly; when I need interaction or layout, I use views.
d: The view sets itself as the layer's `delegate` and implements `display`/`actionForLayer` hooks, which is how UIKit routes `draw(_:)` and, importantly, how it disables implicit animations for view-backed layers (the view returns `NSNull` from `action(for:forKey:)` outside an animation block). You can change the backing layer class by overriding `layerClass` (e.g. return `CAGradientLayer.self`) so the view is backed by a specialized layer. Layers form their own tree (`sublayers`) parallel to the view tree; a view's layer's sublayers include its subviews' layers. Layers store their own copy of geometry and have extras views lack: `cornerRadius`, `borderWidth`, `shadowPath`, `mask`, `contents`. Because layers animate implicitly, mutating a standalone (non-view-backed) layer's property triggers a default animation unless wrapped in `CATransaction` with actions disabled — a frequent source of unexpected animations. Layout-wise, layers have no Auto Layout; you position sublayers manually in `layoutSubviews`/`layoutSublayers(of:)`.
f: Trap: 'Setting `myView.layer.frame` directly is a good way to move the view.' Risk? It's fought by the view: Auto Layout and the view's own frame projection overwrite the layer frame at the next layout pass, and you bypass `center`/`bounds` semantics. Move the view, not its layer.

q: Explain implicit vs explicit Core Animation, and the difference between the model layer and presentation layer.
a: Implicit animations happen automatically when you change an animatable property on a standalone `CALayer` — Core Animation interpolates from old to new value using a default action. Explicit animations are when you create a `CAAnimation` (like `CABasicAnimation`) and add it to a layer, giving you full control over duration, timing, and keyframes. The model layer holds the layer's actual property values — the 'truth' you set — while the presentation layer is a read-only snapshot of what's currently on screen mid-animation. So during an animation, the model layer already has the final value while the presentation layer reflects the interpolated, visible state.
d: When you add an explicit `CAAnimation`, by default it does NOT change the model value — it only overrides the presentation during the animation and is removed on completion (`isRemovedOnCompletion`), causing the infamous 'snap back' unless you also set the model property or set `fillMode = .forwards` with `isRemovedOnCompletion = false` (which leaks animation objects and detaches presentation from model). View-backed layers suppress implicit animations via the delegate returning a null action, which is why `view.layer.cornerRadius = 8` outside an animation block doesn't animate. Hit testing and `presentationLayer`-aware code matter for interactive animations: to detect a tap on a moving view you must hit-test against `layer.presentation()`, because the model frame is already at the destination. `UIViewPropertyAnimator` and `UIView.animate` ultimately create explicit `CAAnimation`s but also update the model values, which is why view animations 'stick.' Timing is controlled by `CAMediaTimingFunction`; the render server, not the app, advances the animation per display refresh.
f: Trap: 'My `CABasicAnimation` jumps back to the start when it finishes.' Cause? It only animated the presentation, not the model value, and was removed on completion. Set the model property to the final value (or use `fillMode`/`isRemovedOnCompletion`, with caveats).

q: How does cell reuse work in `UITableView`/`UICollectionView`, and what is `prepareForReuse` for?
a: Instead of creating a cell per row, the table maintains a reuse pool keyed by identifier. When a cell scrolls off screen it's enqueued, and when a new row needs a cell you call `dequeueReusableCell(withIdentifier:)`, which hands back a recycled instance if one is available or creates a fresh one. This keeps memory bounded to roughly the number of visible cells regardless of dataset size. `prepareForReuse` is called on a cell just before it's handed back from the pool, so it's where I reset transient state — cancel image loads, clear text, reset selection — that shouldn't bleed from the previous row.
d: With the registration-based dequeue APIs (`register(_:forCellReuseIdentifier:)` plus the non-optional `dequeueReusableCell(withIdentifier:for:)`), UIKit guarantees a non-nil cell and handles creation, so you never check for nil. The reuse pool is per identifier; using distinct identifiers for genuinely different cell layouts avoids reconfiguring, but too many identifiers defeats reuse. `prepareForReuse` should be cheap and is explicitly NOT the place to set content (that's the data source's `cellForRow`); Apple recommends only resetting things not related to content, and notes that doing full reconfiguration there can hurt performance. The classic bug is asynchronous image loading: a slow load completes after the cell has been reused for a different row, so you must capture the indexPath/identity and verify or cancel in `prepareForReuse`. Modern `UICollectionView` adds `UICollectionView.CellRegistration` which moves configuration into a closure and pairs well with diffable data sources, reducing reuse-state bugs. Self-sizing cells add a wrinkle: the cell is laid out to compute its size, so constraints must be unambiguous in the cell's content view.
f: Trap: 'Async image set in `cellForRow` is fine because each cell is unique.' Why not? Cells are recycled; a late image callback can land on a cell now showing a different row. Capture identity and cancel/guard in the completion or `prepareForReuse`.

q: Explain diffable data sources and snapshots, and why item identity matters.
a: A diffable data source like `UICollectionViewDiffableDataSource` replaces the manual `numberOfItems`/`cellForItem` bookkeeping with a declarative model: you build an `NSDiffableDataSourceSnapshot` describing the sections and items you want, apply it, and UIKit computes the diff and animates inserts, deletes, and moves for you. The crucial part is that items and sections are identified by `Hashable` identifiers, not by index, so the framework matches old and new state by identity to figure out what actually changed. This eliminates a whole class of 'inconsistency' crashes that came from mismatched batch updates.
d: Identifiers must be stable and unique: if two items hash-equal, the snapshot is invalid and you get a runtime error; if an identifier changes when only the item's content changed, the framework treats it as a delete+insert instead of an update. That's the central subtlety — using the whole model struct as the identifier means any field change looks like a new item, causing unwanted reload animations and losing selection; the fix is to use a stable ID (e.g. the model's `id`) and reconfigure cells for content changes via `reconfigureItems`/`reloadItems`. `apply(_:animatingDifferences:)` must be called consistently; pre-iOS 15 it had to be on the main thread, and applying from a background queue is supported only if you always apply from the same queue. `reconfigureItems` (iOS 15+) updates a visible cell in place without re-dequeuing, which is far cheaper than `reloadItems`. Snapshots are value types you mutate and apply; the data source holds the current snapshot, and `itemIdentifier(for:)`/`indexPath(for:)` bridge between identity and position.
f: Trap: 'I'll use my model struct directly as the diffable item identifier.' Consequence? Any property change alters the hash, so the diff sees delete+insert and re-animates/loses selection. Identify by a stable ID; use `reconfigureItems` for content updates.

q: What is `UICollectionViewCompositionalLayout` and how does it differ from `UICollectionViewFlowLayout`?
a: `UICollectionViewCompositionalLayout` is a composable, declarative layout API where you build layouts from a hierarchy of items inside groups inside sections, each sized with flexible `NSCollectionLayoutDimension` values like fractional, absolute, or estimated. Unlike `UICollectionViewFlowLayout`, which is essentially a single line-based flow with limited customization, compositional layout lets each section have a totally different structure — grids, orthogonally scrolling carousels, badges, headers — without writing a custom `UICollectionViewLayout` subclass. It also natively supports per-section orthogonal scrolling and supplementary/decoration items in a clean way.
d: The model is item -> group -> section -> layout. Groups arrange items horizontally or vertically and can nest, enabling complex mosaics declaratively. Dimensions: `.fractionalWidth/Height` are relative to the container, `.absolute` is fixed points, and `.estimated` enables self-sizing where the actual size is measured at display time (important: estimated sizes trigger a relayout once real sizes are known, so wrong estimates cause visible jumps or extra work). Per-section orthogonal scrolling is set via `orthogonalScrollingBehavior`, giving Netflix-style rows essentially for free, including paging and centered modes. `NSCollectionLayoutSection.visibleItemsInvalidationHandler` provides scroll-position callbacks for parallax/scaling effects without a custom layout. It pairs naturally with diffable data sources, and `UICollectionLayoutListConfiguration` (iOS 14+) provides table-view-like list sections including swipe actions and headers. Compared to a hand-rolled `UICollectionViewLayout`, it handles invalidation, supplementary geometry, and self-sizing correctly, which are the hardest parts to get right manually.
f: Trap: 'Compositional layout requires iOS 13 and only works with diffable data sources.' Accurate? It needs iOS 13+, but it works with a classic data source too — diffable is recommended, not required. The two are complementary, independent APIs.

q: Why must all UIKit work happen on the main thread, and what breaks if it doesn't?
a: UIKit objects are not thread-safe — their internal state, the layer transaction system, and the run-loop-driven layout/display passes all assume single-threaded access on the main thread. If you mutate views off the main thread you get data races: undefined behavior ranging from visual glitches and missed updates to crashes, because two threads can corrupt shared layer state or commit overlapping CoreAnimation transactions. The reason it's the main thread specifically is that's where the main run loop lives and where the CoreAnimation commit observer fires. The fix is always to hop back with `DispatchQueue.main.async` for any UI mutation.
d: The CoreAnimation transaction is per-thread, and view-backed layer mutations enqueue into the main thread's transaction that commits at run-loop end; off-main mutations either land in a transaction that never commits or race with the main one. The Main Thread Checker (a runtime tool) instruments UIKit/AppKit to catch off-main calls and is invaluable; it surfaces issues that otherwise appear nondeterministically. Reads can be just as dangerous as writes because properties are nonatomic and computed. A few specific things commonly bite people: `UIImage` is generally safe to create off-main and even decode off-main, and constructing strings is fine, but assigning to a view, calling `setNeedsLayout`, or touching `UIApplication` must be on main. Networking and JSON parsing should stay off-main; only the final UI assignment hops back. With Swift concurrency, marking UI types `@MainActor` (UIKit is largely annotated now) gives compile-time enforcement instead of runtime crashes. The cost of `DispatchQueue.main.async` is a run-loop hop, so batching UI updates avoids flooding the main queue.
f: Trap: 'Reading `view.frame` from a background thread is harmless since it's just a read.' True? No — properties are nonatomic and the layer state can be mid-mutation; reads can return torn values or race. All UIKit access, reads included, belongs on the main thread.

q: Walk through gesture recognizer states and how delegate methods control conflicts.
a: A gesture recognizer is a state machine. Discrete recognizers like tap go from `.possible` to `.recognized` (then reset), while continuous ones like pan go `.possible`, `.began`, `.changed` repeatedly, then `.ended` or `.cancelled`. UIKit drives these states as it interprets the raw touches, and your action method fires on the relevant transitions. To resolve conflicts between multiple recognizers, I use `UIGestureRecognizerDelegate` methods: `shouldRecognizeSimultaneouslyWith` to allow two to fire together, `shouldRequireFailureOf`/`shouldBeRequiredToFailBy` to order them, and `gestureRecognizerShouldBegin` or `shouldReceiveTouch` to gate recognition.
d: By default recognizers are mutually exclusive only when they'd both claim the same touch; otherwise UIKit lets one win and cancels the others by sending `touchesCancelled` to the view. `require(toFail:)` (and the delegate failure methods) establishes dependencies — e.g. a double-tap requires the single-tap to fail, introducing a deliberate delay for the single tap. `cancelsTouchesInView` (default true) means once a recognizer recognizes, the underlying view's `touchesBegan/Moved` are cancelled — turning it off lets the view see touches too. `delaysTouchesBegan`/`delaysTouchesEnded` control whether touch delivery to the view is held pending recognition, affecting responsiveness. A common real-world conflict is a pan or tap inside a scroll view: scroll views have their own private `panGestureRecognizer`, and you often add your recognizer with `shouldRecognizeSimultaneouslyWith` returning true, or set `require(toFail:)` so the scroll wins. Returning false from `gestureRecognizerShouldBegin` is the cleanest way to conditionally veto a gesture after the system has gathered enough touch data (e.g. only allow horizontal pans).
f: Trap: 'Adding a tap recognizer to a table cell's content breaks scrolling.' Why, and fix? The tap can swallow or delay touches; cells already rely on the table's selection/scroll gestures. Implement `shouldRecognizeSimultaneouslyWith` or prefer `didSelectRowAt` rather than a custom tap.

q: Explain `UIScrollView` content size, content offset, and content inset.
a: `contentSize` is the size of the scrollable content — it tells the scroll view how far it can scroll. `contentOffset` is the current scroll position, specifically the point of the content that's at the top-left of the visible bounds; scrolling is literally changing this offset. `contentInset` adds padding around the content inside the scroll view, so you can, for example, keep content from sitting under a navigation bar or keyboard. Together they define the scrollable region: the offset ranges from negative inset to contentSize minus bounds plus inset.
d: Internally a scroll view scrolls by shifting its `bounds.origin` — `contentOffset` is essentially `bounds.origin` — which is why subviews appear to move without the scroll view itself moving. `adjustedContentInset` (iOS 11+) combines your `contentInset` with the safe area insets when `contentInsetAdjustmentBehavior` is `.automatic`, which is the modern source of confusion when content unexpectedly shifts under bars; set the behavior to `.never` to take full control. With Auto Layout you typically constrain subviews to the scroll view's `contentLayoutGuide` to define `contentSize` and to its `frameLayoutGuide` for fixed-to-frame elements. `contentSize` must be set (or implied by constraints) or the view won't scroll. Scroll indicators have their own `scrollIndicatorInsets`/`verticalScrollIndicatorInsets`. The keyboard-avoidance pattern adjusts `contentInset.bottom` (and `scrollIndicatorInsets`) by the keyboard height. Deceleration, paging, and bounce are all expressed as constraints/animations on the offset, and `setContentOffset(_:animated:)` vs assigning `contentOffset` differ in whether it animates and triggers delegate callbacks.
f: Trap: 'My table content starts under the nav bar even though I set `contentInset` to zero.' Why? `adjustedContentInset` adds the safe area automatically. Either rely on it or set `contentInsetAdjustmentBehavior = .never` and manage insets yourself.

q: What is the safe area, and how does it differ from layout margins?
a: The safe area is the region of a view not covered by system UI like the status bar, navigation bar, home indicator, or device notches and curved corners — you constrain content to the `safeAreaLayoutGuide` so it stays visible and uninterrupted. Layout margins, exposed via `layoutMarginsGuide` and `directionalLayoutMargins`, are about consistent internal padding between a view's edges and its content, independent of system chrome. So safe area answers 'where is it safe to put content,' while layout margins answer 'how much breathing room do I want inside this view.' They can compose: you might pin to the safe area and then inset further with margins.
d: `safeAreaInsets` are propagated down the hierarchy and you can read or react to changes via `safeAreaInsetsDidChange`. A view controller can add to the safe area for its children with `additionalSafeAreaInsets`, useful when you overlay a custom toolbar. Layout margins are directional (`directionalLayoutMargins` uses leading/trailing for RTL support) and `preservesSuperviewLayoutMargins` plus `insetsLayoutMarginsFromSafeArea` (default true) control how margins inherit and whether they automatically incorporate safe area insets — the latter being a frequent gotcha where margins are larger than expected near screen edges. `systemMinimumLayoutMargins` enforces a baseline on view controllers' root views. Before iOS 11 there was `topLayoutGuide`/`bottomLayoutGuide`, now deprecated in favor of the safe area guide. Using `safeAreaLayoutGuide` is essential for notch/Dynamic Island and home-indicator devices; pinning directly to edges (`view.topAnchor`) puts content under bars. The safe area is dynamic — it changes with rotation, bar visibility, and keyboard in some contexts — so constraints to it re-resolve automatically.
f: Trap: 'Pinning to `view.topAnchor` is the same as the safe area on a notchless phone, so it's fine.' Risk? It breaks on notch/Dynamic Island devices and under nav bars. Always pin to `safeAreaLayoutGuide.topAnchor` for content meant to stay clear of chrome.

q: How does `UINavigationController` manage its stack, and how do push/pop work?
a: A `UINavigationController` owns an ordered array of view controllers — the navigation stack — where the last one is visible. `pushViewController(_:animated:)` adds a controller to the top with a slide animation, and `popViewController` removes the top one; there's also `popToRootViewController` and `setViewControllers(_:animated:)` to replace the stack wholesale. The navigation bar updates automatically from each controller's `navigationItem`, and the interactive back swipe is driven by the `interactivePopGestureRecognizer`. Each push/pop triggers the appropriate appearance lifecycle callbacks on the controllers involved.
d: The nav controller is a container view controller: pushed controllers become its children, and it forwards appearance and trait callbacks. Pushing retains the controller in `viewControllers`; popping releases it, so a popped controller deallocates unless something else retains it — a common leak source is a retain cycle between a popped controller and a closure/delegate. The back button comes from the previous controller's `navigationItem.backBarButtonItem` / `title`, set on the controller being pushed-over, which surprises people who set it on the wrong controller. `interactivePopGestureRecognizer` can break if you set a custom back button or hide the nav bar; you often set its delegate or re-enable it. `setViewControllers(_:animated:)` lets you rewrite history (e.g. remove an intermediate step) without visible intermediate transitions. Appearance order during push is roughly: outgoing `viewWillDisappear`, incoming `viewWillAppear`, then after the transition the `did` variants — and an interactive pop that's cancelled fires will-appear/disappear pairs that don't reflect the final state.
f: Trap: 'After I pop a controller it stays alive until a memory warning.' Likely cause? A retain cycle (e.g. a strong delegate or closure capturing self), not normal behavior. Popping releases the stack reference; if it survives, break the cycle with `weak`/`unowned`.

q: Describe building a custom transition with `UIViewControllerAnimatedTransitioning`.
a: You provide a transitioning delegate that vends an animator object conforming to `UIViewControllerAnimatedTransitioning`. That animator implements `transitionDuration` and `animateTransition(using:)`, where you're handed a `UIViewControllerContextTransitioning` giving you the from- and to-view controllers, their views, and the `containerView` to animate within. You add the destination view to the container, animate it into place, and crucially call `completeTransition(_:)` when done so UIKit can finalize the view hierarchy. For interactive transitions you also supply a `UIViewControllerInteractiveTransitioning` (typically `UIPercentDrivenInteractiveTransition`) driven by a gesture.
d: The `containerView` is a transient superview UIKit creates for the transition; you must add the to-view to it yourself, and use the context's `view(forKey:)`/`viewController(forKey:)` and `finalFrame(for:)` rather than assuming geometry — `finalFrame` accounts for bars and safe area. Forgetting `completeTransition(transitionContext.transitionWasCancelled == false)` leaves the UI wedged; for interactive transitions you must pass the cancellation state so UIKit removes/keeps the right views. Interactive transitions update progress via `update(_:)` and resolve with `finish()`/`cancel()`, and you should use `UIViewPropertyAnimator`-based interruptible transitions for smooth scrubbing. For navigation, you set the nav controller's `delegate` and implement `animationController(for:)` and `interactionControllerFor:`; for modals you set the presented controller's `transitioningDelegate`. A subtlety: during a cancelled interactive transition, lifecycle and the view hierarchy must be restored exactly, which is why relying on `completeTransition` with the correct boolean is non-negotiable. `UIViewControllerContextTransitioning`'s `containerView` may already contain the from-view, so don't blindly remove things.
f: Trap: 'My custom transition shows the animation but then the screen is frozen/black.' Most likely bug? You didn't call `completeTransition(_:)` (or passed the wrong cancelled flag). UIKit needs that call to commit the final view hierarchy.

q: How does view controller containment work, and what are the required calls for child VCs?
a: Containment lets you embed one view controller's content inside another so the child still gets proper lifecycle, rotation, and trait callbacks. The required sequence to add a child is: call `addChild(child)`, add the child's view to your hierarchy, then call `child.didMove(toParent: self)`. To remove, you call `child.willMove(toParent: nil)`, remove its view, then `child.removeFromParent()`. Getting this dance right is what guarantees the child receives `viewWillAppear`, memory warnings, and trait changes instead of being a detached, lifecycle-less view.
d: The asymmetry is deliberate: `addChild` itself calls `willMove(toParent:)` on the child, so you only manually call `didMove(toParent:)` after inserting the view; conversely you manually call `willMove(toParent: nil)` before removal, and `removeFromParent` calls `didMove(toParent: nil)`. If you skip these, appearance callbacks may not forward correctly, and `shouldAutomaticallyForwardAppearanceMethods` (default true) governs whether the parent auto-forwards `viewWillAppear` etc.; if you return false you must call `beginAppearanceTransition`/`endAppearanceTransition` yourself. Containment is the foundation of `UINavigationController`, `UITabBarController`, and `UIPageViewController`, and it's how you build custom containers. Constraint-wise you typically pin the child's view to the parent's bounds or safe area after adding. A common bug is adding the child view but forgetting `didMove`, which leaves the parent/child relationship half-formed and can suppress rotation/trait propagation. The child's `parent` property reflects the relationship and is how the child finds its container.
f: Trap: 'I added the child's view as a subview, so it's a proper child VC.' Sufficient? No — without `addChild`/`didMove(toParent:)` it's an orphaned view; lifecycle, traits, and rotation won't forward. The containment API calls are mandatory.

q: Explain how `UIStackView` lays out its arranged subviews.
a: `UIStackView` is a non-rendering container that arranges its `arrangedSubviews` along an axis using Auto Layout constraints it generates for you, based on its `axis`, `distribution`, `alignment`, and `spacing`. It doesn't draw anything itself — it just creates and manages the constraints that position and size the arranged views, relying heavily on their intrinsic content sizes and content hugging/compression priorities. The distribution decides how space along the axis is shared (fill, fillEqually, fillProportionally, equalSpacing, equalCentering), and alignment controls the perpendicular axis (fill, leading, center, etc.). Hiding an arranged subview makes the stack reclaim its space automatically.
d: Arranged subviews are a separate concept from `subviews`: `addArrangedSubview` both adds to the subviews and registers it for layout, while plain `addSubview` puts a view in without arranging it. The stack resolves ambiguity using the arranged views' hugging/compression priorities — `fillProportionally` sizes by intrinsic content ratio, `fillEqually` ignores intrinsic sizes for equal sizing. Setting `isHidden = true` on an arranged subview triggers an animatable layout where the stack removes that view's space; animating that inside `layoutIfNeeded` gives smooth collapse. `spacing` can be customized per-gap with `setCustomSpacing(_:after:)`. Because the stack relies on intrinsic sizes, views without intrinsic size (like a bare `UIView`) need explicit constraints or they collapse to zero on the axis. Deeply nested stacks are convenient but multiply constraint count and can hurt performance in cells; for hot paths a manual layout or compositional layout may be cheaper. The stack's own size is driven by its content unless you constrain it.
f: Trap: 'To remove a view from a stack, call `removeFromSuperview`.' Complete? That removes it from layout too, but to just stop arranging without removing, you'd use `removeArrangedSubview` (which leaves it as a subview). And to temporarily hide-with-collapse, set `isHidden = true` instead.

q: What is the cost of `UIImage` decoding, and how do you decode off the main thread?
a: A `UIImage` created from data or a file holds compressed bytes; the expensive decode — turning JPEG/PNG into a raw bitmap — is deferred until the image is first drawn, which by default happens on the main thread during the display pass and causes scroll jank. The fix is to force-decode the image ahead of time on a background queue so the main thread only composites already-decoded pixels. On iOS 15+ you can use `UIImage.byPreparingForDisplay()` / `prepareForDisplay(completionHandler:)` which does exactly this; otherwise you render the image into a bitmap context yourself.
d: Lazy decode means a thumbnail grid can stall the main thread the first time each cell appears, even though loading 'finished' earlier. Pre-decoding draws the image into a `CGContext` (or uses `UIGraphicsImageRenderer`) to materialize the bitmap; better still, downsample at decode time using `CGImageSourceCreateThumbnailAtIndex` with `kCGImageSourceCreateThumbnailFromImageAlways` and `kCGImageSourceThumbnailMaxPixelSize`, so you never hold a full-resolution bitmap for a small image view — this slashes memory, since decoded size is width times height times 4 bytes regardless of file compression. `byPreparingThumbnail(ofSize:)` is the modern convenience for downsampling. Match the decode size to the on-screen point size times `scale` to avoid both over-allocation and blurriness. Caching decoded images (e.g. `NSCache`) avoids repeat decodes. Note that asset catalog images and `UIImage(named:)` are cached and decoded by the system, but large user/network images are where this bites. Off-main decoding plus correct downsampling is the single biggest win for image-heavy scrolling.
f: Trap: 'Loading the image data on a background thread fixes scroll jank.' Enough? No — data loading isn't the decode. The decode still happens lazily on the main thread at draw time. Force-decode/downsample off-main (`prepareForDisplay`/thumbnail APIs).

q: How do trait collections drive adaptive layout, and how do you respond to changes?
a: A `UITraitCollection` describes the environment — size classes (compact/regular width and height), display scale, user interface idiom, dynamic type size, dark vs light appearance, and more. UIKit propagates traits down the view controller and view hierarchy, and you adapt your layout based on them, for example switching from a side-by-side to a stacked layout when width is compact. When traits change — rotation, multitasking resize, appearance switch — UIKit notifies you so you can update constraints or appearance. Historically that was `traitCollectionDidChange`, and on iOS 17+ the preferred mechanism is the trait change registration API.
d: Size classes are the key adaptive primitive: an iPhone in portrait is compact width/regular height, most iPads and landscape phones differ, and crucially Split View/Slide Over on iPad changes size classes at runtime, so you can't assume idiom equals layout. `traitCollectionDidChange(_:)` is deprecated in iOS 17 in favor of `registerForTraitChanges(_:handler:)`, which is more efficient because UIKit only calls you for the specific traits you registered for rather than on every change. Dark mode resolves dynamic colors and images via the trait collection, so reading `UIColor`'s CGColor for a layer must be done with `resolvedColor(with:)` or re-resolved on trait change — a common dark-mode bug where layer borders don't update. `UITraitCollection.current` lets you resolve dynamic assets imperatively. Interface Builder lets you vary constraints/fonts by size class. `overrideUserInterfaceStyle` forces light/dark on a subtree. Dynamic Type is also a trait (`preferredContentSizeCategory`), and supporting it means using text styles and reacting to its changes.
f: Trap: 'iPad means regular size class, iPhone means compact, so I branch on idiom.' Flaw? On iPad, Split View/Slide Over yields compact width; on a Plus/Max phone landscape differs too. Branch on size class, not idiom.

q: What causes offscreen rendering and blending overhead, and how do you avoid them for fast cells?
a: Offscreen rendering is when the GPU has to render content into a separate buffer before compositing it to screen, which is expensive — classic triggers are layer masks, rounded corners combined with `masksToBounds` plus shadows, and rasterization. Blending overhead comes from compositing translucent (non-opaque) layers, where the GPU must read what's behind and blend, costing fill-rate that hurts scrolling on big lists. For fast cells I make views opaque with solid backgrounds, avoid expensive corner-radius-plus-shadow combinations, and pre-render or flatten where possible. The Core Animation instrument's color flags (color blended layers, color offscreen-rendered) let me find these visually.
d: Rounded corners alone via `cornerRadius` are cheap on modern hardware, but combining `cornerRadius` + `masksToBounds` + a shadow forces an offscreen pass; using `shadowPath` (a precomputed CGPath) avoids the offscreen render the shadow would otherwise need because the system no longer has to compute the shape from the alpha channel. `shouldRasterize` caches a layer's rendering as a bitmap — a win for static content reused across frames, but a trap for content that changes every frame (it re-rasterizes constantly) or at the wrong `rasterizationScale` (blurry/re-rendered). Blending: set `isOpaque = true` and provide a fully opaque `backgroundColor`; transparent labels over images, or `alpha < 1`, force blending. Overlapping translucent layers compound fill-rate cost. For images, pre-rounding the bitmap (drawing into a rounded context) avoids per-frame masking. Excessive subview/layer count and large `draw(_:)` backing stores also hurt. The Debug menu's 'Color Blended Layers' (red) and 'Color Offscreen-Rendered Yellow' overlays in the simulator/Instruments are the diagnostic tools; the goal in a scroll path is green (opaque, no offscreen).
f: Trap: 'Adding a shadow to a rounded cell is fine performance-wise.' Sure? Shadow + masking forces offscreen rendering and re-computes the shape each frame. Set an explicit `shadowPath` so the GPU skips the offscreen pass.

q: What are the common `prepareForReuse` pitfalls?
a: The biggest pitfall is doing too much or too little. Putting content configuration in `prepareForReuse` instead of in the data source's `cellForRow` is wrong — Apple says reset only non-content state there and configure content in `cellForRow`, because `prepareForReuse` runs on the recycle path and over-doing it hurts scroll performance. The opposite pitfall is forgetting to reset transient state, so leftover data from the previous row bleeds through — a stale image, a highlighted state, an in-flight network/image task, or KVO/notification observers that should be torn down. The subtle one is async work: an image or data callback completing after reuse lands on the wrong cell.
d: `prepareForReuse` is called only on dequeued (reused) cells, never on freshly created ones, so any reset logic there must be mirrored in initialization or you'll have inconsistent first-vs-reused state. Cancelling in-flight async tasks here is important, but the more robust pattern is identity verification at completion time (capture the indexPath/model id and check the cell still represents it) plus a token, because `prepareForReuse` may fire after the new `cellForRow` has already kicked off a new request. Don't reset `accessoryType` or selection if you intend the table to manage it; conversely, do clear delegate/closure references that capture the previous row. Animations in progress should be removed (`layer.removeAllAnimations`) to avoid carrying motion into the new content. With diffable data sources and `CellRegistration`/`reconfigureItems`, much of this is handled because configuration is centralized, reducing reliance on `prepareForReuse`. Resetting expensive resources (gradients, attributed strings) unnecessarily every reuse is itself a perf bug.
f: Trap: 'I'll move all my cell setup into `prepareForReuse` so it's in one place.' Why bad? It runs on the reuse path and isn't called for new cells, causing perf hits and first-cell inconsistency. Configure content in `cellForRow`; only reset transient state in `prepareForReuse`.

q: How should an app handle memory warnings in UIKit?
a: When the system is under memory pressure it sends a memory warning — `didReceiveMemoryWarning` on view controllers and a corresponding notification/app-delegate callback — and your job is to release anything you can recreate, like caches, decoded images, and offscreen data. You should drop large in-memory buffers and let `NSCache` purge, but never release state you can't rebuild or that's needed by the visible UI. The goal is to lower your footprint enough to avoid being jetsammed (killed) by the OS. Heavy resource holders like image caches should subscribe to the warning and evict proactively.
d: On modern iOS, view controllers' views are no longer automatically unloaded on memory warnings (that pre-iOS 6 `viewDidUnload` behavior is gone), so `didReceiveMemoryWarning` is mainly your hook to free caches. `NSCache` already evicts under pressure automatically and is thread-safe, which is why it's preferred over a plain dictionary for image caches. The warning is a courtesy — the real enforcement is the jetsam mechanism, which kills the highest-memory background apps first, so foreground responsiveness depends on staying lean. You can observe `UIApplication.didReceiveMemoryWarningNotification` from non-controller objects. Don't do heavy synchronous work in the handler; it's called on the main thread and the system is already stressed. The most effective prevention is not generating the warning at all: downsample images to display size, avoid retaining full-resolution bitmaps, and watch for leaks/retain cycles (which warnings won't help since you can't free leaked memory). Tools: Instruments Allocations/Leaks and the memory graph debugger. Note simulator's 'Simulate Memory Warning' helps test handlers.
f: Trap: 'On a memory warning I'll release my data model to free RAM.' Wise? No — releasing non-recreatable, in-use state causes crashes or data loss. Free only caches and regenerable resources; leaked memory isn't reclaimable this way anyway — fix the leak.

q: Compare the view lifecycle to the controller lifecycle and how layout fits between them.
a: The controller lifecycle is about a `UIViewController`'s phases — loading its view, appearing, disappearing — while the view lifecycle is about an individual `UIView` being added to a hierarchy, moving to a window, laying out, drawing, and being removed. They're related but distinct: a view can be created, laid out, and drawn independently of any controller, and a controller orchestrates its root view's participation in the window. Layout sits between them as a recurring pass: the controller's `viewWillLayoutSubviews`/`viewDidLayoutSubviews` bracket the view tree's `layoutSubviews` calls, which themselves bracket any `draw(_:)`. So a single appearance can drive one controller-load but many layout and draw passes.
d: View-side callbacks include `willMove(toWindow:)`/`didMoveToWindow`, `willMove(toSuperview:)`/`didMoveToSuperview`, `layoutSubviews`, `draw(_:)`, and `removeFromSuperview`; `didMoveToWindow` with a non-nil window is the view-level analog of 'I'm now in a live hierarchy' and is a good place for window-dependent setup (it can fire before the controller's `viewDidAppear`). The controller's appearance callbacks are triggered by the container, whereas the view's window callbacks are triggered structurally by hierarchy mutations, so they don't map one-to-one — e.g. `didMoveToWindow` happens during the push transition's setup, before `viewDidAppear`. The layout pass is the integration point: Auto Layout (driven by the run loop) calls `viewWillLayoutSubviews` on the controller, then `layoutSubviews` top-down on views, then `viewDidLayoutSubviews`. Because layout can run many times (rotation, safe-area changes, scrolling, keyboard), separating one-time controller setup (`viewDidLoad`) from per-layout view work is essential. A view removed and re-added gets new window callbacks but the controller may not re-run `viewDidLoad`.
f: Trap: 'Window-dependent setup belongs in `viewDidLoad`.' Always safe? No — in `viewDidLoad` the view isn't in a window yet (`view.window` is nil). Use `didMoveToWindow` (or `viewWillAppear`/`viewDidAppear`) for anything needing a live window.

q: How do self-sizing cells and estimated row heights work in `UITableView`, and what are the performance implications?
a: Self-sizing cells let the table compute each row's height from Auto Layout instead of you returning a number — you set `rowHeight = UITableView.automaticDimension` and provide an `estimatedRowHeight`, and the cell sizes itself by satisfying its content view constraints with `systemLayoutSizeFitting`. The estimate matters for performance: the table uses it to size the scroll indicator and content size without laying out every cell up front, so a good estimate avoids expensive full-pass measurement and prevents scroll-position jumps. The real height is computed lazily as each cell comes into view. The cell's content view constraints must form an unambiguous chain from top to bottom for the height to resolve.
d: Without estimated heights, the table calls `heightForRow` for every row before displaying anything to compute `contentSize`, which is O(n) and janky for large datasets; a non-zero estimate makes that O(visible) by trusting the estimate and correcting on the fly. The correction is the cause of the notorious 'scroll indicator jumps' or content offset shifts when real heights diverge from estimates, especially when scrolling up into already-passed rows — keeping estimates close to reality minimizes this. `systemLayoutSizeFitting(_:withHorizontalFittingPriority:verticalFittingPriority:)` is what UIKit calls; for a vertically-growing cell you pass the target width with `.required` horizontal and `.fittingSizeLevel` vertical priority. Multi-line labels need their width pinned so `preferredMaxLayoutWidth` resolves, or heights come out wrong. Caching computed heights by indexPath/identity avoids recomputation on reload. `UICollectionView` has the analogous `estimatedItemSize`/`UICollectionViewFlowLayout.automaticSize` and compositional layout's `.estimated` dimension. Heavy constraint solving per cell is itself a cost, so very complex cells may be faster with manual `sizeThatFits`. `invalidateIntrinsicContentSize` or `setNeedsLayout` on dynamic content (e.g. after async image load changing height) requires telling the table to re-measure, often via `performBatchUpdates`/`beginUpdates`.
f: Trap: 'Setting `estimatedRowHeight` to a small constant like 1 is harmless.' Effect? Wildly wrong estimates cause large content-size errors and visible scroll jumps/offset shifts during scrolling. Provide a realistic estimate (or cache actual heights).
